lyrics:
I've been jealous of younger faces
And embarrassed that I was
I've been sharp with happy people
Just because they still had buzz
I've made little jokes about myself
So nobody else could get there first
And called that being honest
When really I was hurt

I don't love these parts
But there they are
And lying doesn't wash them off

I've got bitter in me
I've got fear in me
I've got pride that hates growing old
I've got anger, vanity
Old regret and self-pity
I don't say that proud
I just say it straight
It's in me too

I have judged the young for ease
As if I wouldn't wear it too
I have turned my losses holy
Just to make them feel more true
I have used my pain like proof
That I see deeper than the rest
Maybe sometimes that was wisdom
Other times just defense

So here's the hard part
No nice light
No better story than the one that fits

I've got bitter in me
I've got fear in me
I've got pride that hates growing old
I've got anger, vanity
Old regret and self-pity
I don't say that proud
I just say it straight
It's in me too

And maybe saying all of this
Out loud for once
Keeps it from running everything
The way it does some days
(yeah)
Some days

I've got bitter in me
I've got fear in me
I've got things I wish had never grown this way
Still they're mine to face
Mine to keep in place
Mine to keep from running every room I'm in
I don't need to hide
I can say it plain
It's in me too

impression.text: This track establishes a vulnerable and confessional atmosphere with a sparse, intimate texture centered on a clean electric guitar and a close-miked male vocal. It has a dramatic dynamic arc, building from a low-energy, reflective verse into a powerful, anthemic rock chorus that feels cathartic and expansive. The production is clear and spacious, allowing individual elements to breathe in the verses while expertly layering guitars and background vocals for a dense, wide soundscape in the louder sections without losing vocal clarity. The most defining quality is the stark transition into the chorus, where quiet self-analysis erupts into a raw, full-band declaration, a shift that provides the song's primary emotional and structural hook.

review.review_text: Rose has written self-examination songs before, but 'It's in Me Too' is the one where the ledger gets read out loud without flinching: 'I've used my pain like proof / That I see deeper than the rest.' That kind of honesty earns the big chorus it gets, and the production delivers, a close-miked confession in B major that breaks open into full-band declaration right when the lyric demands it. The quiet-to-loud architecture here is closer to Springsteen at his most direct than anything indie or country, and it works because the restraint in the verses is real, not strategic. A career-best contender.
anchors_used: lyric quote: 'I've used my pain like proof / That I see deeper than the rest'
